title: SEGMENT 11 — Personalized medicine with tumor organoids: the dream becoming routine

lines.text: SEGMENT 11 — Personalized medicine with tumor organoids: the dream becoming routine

Let's come back to tumor organoids, because in the years since we first touched on them in Segment 4, the technology has moved from cool research demonstration to active clinical application, and I want to spend a segment on how that has happened and what it means.
The core idea is the one I sketched earlier: take a piece of your tumor, grow it as an organoid, test a panel of drugs in parallel, treat you with whichever one shrinks the dish.
That's the dream of personalized cancer medicine, and tumor organoids have made it concretely operationalizable.
Here's how it works in a working clinical context.
A patient is diagnosed with cancer — say, colorectal cancer.
During surgery or biopsy, a small piece of the tumor is collected.
The tissue is sent to a specialized lab that grows tumor organoids.
Within 2 to 6 weeks (depending on the cancer type and the protocol), there's a healthy organoid culture established.
The organoids are characterized to confirm they match the original tumor — by genetics (the same mutations), by histology (the same appearance under the microscope), and by markers.
Once confirmed, the organoids are split into many wells and exposed to a panel of cancer drugs — sometimes a dozen, sometimes a hundred.
The drugs that kill the organoids best are flagged.
Those flagged drugs are reported back to the oncologist, who uses the information along with other clinical considerations to choose treatment.
This is happening now, in real clinics, for real patients.
The Dutch HUB Organoids consortium has run trials of organoid-guided treatment for several cancer types.
The American group around the Wake Forest Institute for Regenerative Medicine has done similar work for colorectal and pancreatic cancers.
In the UK, several NHS trusts are piloting tumor organoid testing for specific cancer indications.

The results, in the published trials so far, have been encouraging — there's typically a strong correlation between organoid drug response and patient drug response, with positive predictive values in some studies above 80%.
The strongest validation has come from cancers where the standard of care includes a relatively well-defined panel of options.
In colorectal cancer, for instance, there are well-established cytotoxic chemotherapy regimens plus several targeted therapies (cetuximab, bevacizumab, regorafenib, and so on).
Testing patient organoids against these options in advance can help oncologists choose the most likely-to-work regimen for each patient.
Multiple studies have shown organoid-based prediction is more accurate than standard genomic biomarkers alone.

In pancreatic cancer, where the prognosis is grim and the available drugs are limited, organoid testing has been used to identify the few patients whose tumors respond to specific regimens — and to spare the rest from cycles of ineffective and toxic treatment.

This is sometimes called "the precision oncology dream" — the idea that we can stop blindly applying generic treatment protocols and start tailoring treatment to each tumor's specific vulnerabilities.

In rare cancers and cancers of unknown primary origin — where there are few standard treatment guidelines because the population of patients is too small to support large clinical trials — tumor organoids may be especially valuable.
If you can't run a clinical trial because there aren't enough patients, you can still test drugs on each patient's organoid and make individualized decisions.
There are important limitations to this approach that I should mention.
First: not all tumors grow well as organoids.
Some cancer types — certain leukemias, some glioblastomas — have established culture systems; others remain difficult.
The success rate for establishing colorectal organoids is around 90%; for some other cancers, it's much lower.
Second: organoids lack the tumor microenvironment.

They're cancer cells, possibly with some stromal cells, but they don't have the immune cells, the surrounding normal tissue, the blood vessels, the systemic factors that influence drug response in a patient.
So a drug that works on the organoid might fail in the patient because the patient's immune system clears the drug before it can act, or because the tumor in vivo is protected by surrounding stroma.
There's active work on patient-derived organoids plus autologous immune cells, where you grow the tumor organoid alongside immune cells isolated from the same patient, to model immune interactions.
This is especially important for immunotherapy drugs, which work by activating the immune response.
Third: time.
The few weeks it takes to establish and test organoids can be a barrier when treatment decisions need to be made urgently.
Faster protocols are being developed, but there's a fundamental tradeoff between speed and reliability.
Fourth: cost and access.
Organoid testing is currently expensive and not widely available.
It's not yet routine for the average cancer patient.
As the technology matures and protocols become standardized, costs should come down, but we're not there yet.
The overall trajectory, though, is unmistakable.
Patient-derived tumor organoids are becoming a tool that real oncologists are starting to use to make real decisions for real patients.

They are bridging the gap between molecular characterization of a tumor (which we've been able to do for years through genomics) and functional characterization (actually testing what the tumor does and responds to).

They are the closest thing we've had to a "preclinical trial in a dish" — a way to anticipate, with reasonable accuracy, what's going to happen in the patient before subjecting the patient to the treatment.
Five years ago, this was research.
Ten years from now, my bet is that some form of patient-derived organoid testing will be standard of care for several major cancers.
It's a remarkable trajectory: from a 2009 mouse intestinal organoid paper to clinical decision-making for thousands of cancer patients, in less than two decades.
